title: Cobaea gracilis
taxonomy: Cobaea gracilis is a species of flowering plant in the family Polemoniaceae. It is a type of morning glory that is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The classification of Cobaea gracilis is as follows: kingdom - Plantae, clade - Angiosperms, clade - Eudicots, clade - Asterids, order - Polemoniales, family - Polemoniaceae, genus - Cobaea, species - C. gracilis. The plant is characterized by its twining stems and funnel-shaped flowers. Cobaea gracilis is a perennial vine that is often cultivated in gardens for its attractive flowers.
uses: Cobaea gracilis is a climbing plant native to the tropical regions of Central and South America, and its medicinal uses are not well-documented. intro: Cobaea gracilis, also known as the 'Lady's Pocket-handkerchief', is a climbing plant species in the morning glory family (Convolvulaceae). It has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including respiratory problems, fever, and inflammation, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Cobaea gracilis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal effects. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often consumed to alleviate symptoms of colds, coughs, and flu.
